county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. County records are well preserved from formation. Some early township records have gaps due to informal record-keeping in rural areas before the rise of Atlantic City.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Gloucester County in 1837. Atlantic City was incorporated in 1854 and became the dominant population center. The county includes the Pine Barrens region with bog iron and glass industry heritage. Hammonton was a major Italian immigrant settlement. The Mullica River communities have roots in the colonial era. Egg Harbor Township records predate county formation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Atlantic County Clerk in Mays Landing holds deed records from 1837. Surrogate's Court has probate from 1837. For pre-1837 records, check Gloucester County. The Atlantic County Historical Society in Somers Point maintains family files, photographs, and Atlantic City resort-era records. The Noyes Museum archives document Pine Barrens communities. Hammonton's Italian heritage is documented in St. Joseph's Church records (from 1873) and the Hammonton Historical Society. For African American genealogy, research the free Black communities of Whitesboro (founded 1901) and the earlier Absecon-area settlements. Atlantic City hotel and boardinghouse employment records at the Atlantic City Free Public Library provide occupation data for the resort era.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed and mortgage records from county formation; online index available through county clerk portal

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from county formation. Includes grantor/grantee indexes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1837. No significant gaps.

source_profiles.source_name: Atlantic County Surrogate's Court — Probate Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and inventories from county formation.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1837. Well-maintained series.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1840 (first census after county formation). Includes population, agriculture, and manufacturing schedules.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete. 1890 census largely destroyed nationally.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Joseph's Roman Catholic Church Records — Hammonton
source_profiles.repository: Diocese of Camden Archives / St. Joseph's Parish
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptism, marriage, and burial records for Hammonton's large Italian-American community. One of the earliest Italian parishes in South Jersey.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. Parish registers maintained continuously.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized newspapers including Atlantic City press coverage, resort-era social columns, and Mays Landing regional papers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Growing digital collection. Additional papers on microfilm at Rutgers.

source_profiles.source_name: NJ Adjutant General Records — Atlantic County Civil War
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil War muster rolls, service records, and pension applications for Atlantic County soldiers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete state-level records. Federal records at NARA.

county_metadata.record_losses: Some early Dutch-era records were lost during the Revolutionary War when British forces occupied Bergen County. However, most colonial records survived through transcription into English after 1664.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the four original East Jersey counties (1683). Bergen was the heart of Dutch settlement in New Jersey — Hackensack, Bergen (now part of Jersey City), and surrounding areas. The county lost territory when Hudson County was created in 1840 and Passaic County in 1837. Dutch Reformed Church records are essential supplements to civil records. Bergen County's Dutch heritage persisted well into the 19th century with Dutch-language records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bergen County Clerk in Hackensack holds deeds from 1683, making it one of the oldest continuous deed series in New Jersey. For Dutch-era research, the Holland Society of New York and the New York Genealogical and Biographical Society have key transcriptions. Bergen County's earliest records are in Dutch — consult 'Documents Relating to the Colonial History of New Jersey' (NJ Archives series). The Bergen County Historical Society in Steuben House, River Edge, has family files and local manuscripts. The Hackensack church (First Reformed, est. 1686) records are critical for colonial families. Check the Johnson Free Public Library in Hackensack for city directories and local newspapers. Many Bergen families had dual New York-New Jersey connections — always cross-reference New York City records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed and mortgage index online; images for recent decades; older records in-person only

source_profiles.source_name: Bergen County Clerk — Deed Records from 1683
source_profiles.coverage_notes: One of the oldest deed series in NJ. Early deeds in Dutch with English translations. Grantor/grantee indexes available.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival from 1683 despite Revolutionary War disruptions. Some early volumes restored.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, inventories, administrations from original county formation. Some early records in Dutch.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival overall. Pre-1785 records have some gaps.

source_profiles.source_name: First Reformed Church of Hackensack Records
source_profiles.repository: First Reformed Church / Gardner Sage Library, New Brunswick
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptism, marriage, and membership records for the oldest Dutch Reformed congregation in Bergen County. Essential for colonial Dutch families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records well preserved. Transcriptions published by the Holland Society.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete federal census from 1790. Bergen County appears in all enumerations. Dutch-surname families predominate in early censuses.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890 (destroyed nationally).

source_profiles.repository: NJ State Archives / NARA New York
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Naturalization petitions and declarations from county courts. Significant for Italian, Polish, and Greek immigrants who settled in Bergen County's industrial towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Reasonable survival. Some gaps in early court records.

source_profiles.source_name: Bergen County Tax Ratables and Assessment Lists
source_profiles.repository: NJ State Archives / Bergen County Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial and early republic tax lists showing property holders, livestock, and acreage. Critical for tracking Dutch-American families before civil vital records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered survival. Best coverage 1770s-1820s.

source_profiles.source_name: Bergen County Democrat and Hackensack Republican Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Johnson Free Public Library, Hackensack / NJ Digital Highway
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Local newspapers with obituaries, legal notices, marriage announcements, and community news for Bergen County towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Microfilm runs at Johnson Library. Partial digitization via Chronicling America.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant losses. Burlington's Quaker tradition of meticulous record-keeping resulted in some of the best-preserved colonial records in New Jersey.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the two original West Jersey counties (1681, with Salem). Burlington was the capital of West Jersey and seat of the West Jersey Proprietors. The West Jersey Proprietors' records, still maintained in Burlington, are critical for colonial land research. English Quakers were the dominant early settlers. Camden County was taken from Burlington in 1844, and Ocean County in 1850. Burlington County is the largest county in NJ by area and includes Pine Barrens communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: The West Jersey Proprietors' office in Burlington City holds original proprietary land records from 1676 — one of the most important colonial land record collections in America. The Burlington County Clerk in Mount Holly has deeds from 1681. Burlington Monthly Meeting (Quaker) records at Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore College, are essential for colonial families. The Burlington County Historical Society in Burlington City has extensive family files. For Pine Barrens research, check the Pinelands Preservation Alliance archives and Batsto Village historical records (ironworks, glass factories). Medford, Moorestown, and Mount Holly have strong local historical societies with pre-Revolutionary materials. The NJ State Archives has Burlington County court records and road returns.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County clerk deed and mortgage records; proprietary records at West Jersey Proprietors office in Burlington

source_profiles.source_name: Burlington County Clerk — Deed Records from 1681
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Among the oldest continuous deed series in NJ. Quaker-era land transactions well documented.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival from 1681. Quaker record-keeping tradition ensured careful preservation.

source_profiles.source_name: West Jersey Proprietors — Proprietary Land Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original proprietary surveys, warrants, and patents for West Jersey. The most important source for early land ownership in the West Jersey division.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Remarkably complete. One of the best-preserved proprietary record sets in colonial America.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, and guardianships from county formation. Early Quaker wills often reference meeting membership.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1681. Among the best-preserved probate records in NJ.

source_profiles.source_name: Burlington Monthly Meeting (Society of Friends) Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Quaker meeting records including births, marriages, deaths, certificates of removal, and disownments. Burlington was a center of West Jersey Quaker life.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent. Quakers were meticulous record keepers. FamilySearch has microfilm copies.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete federal census from 1790. Includes agricultural schedules showing Pine Barrens farming communities.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Militia rolls, oaths of allegiance, and military claims from Burlington County during the Revolution. Burlington was a strategic location on the Delaware River.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival at state archives. Published in NJ Archives series.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax assessment lists showing property owners, acreage, livestock, and enslaved persons. Critical for pre-vital-records genealogy.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered survival. Best coverage for colonial period.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Some Camden City municipal records suffered water damage in 20th century floods but county-level records remain intact.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Gloucester County in 1844. Camden City, directly across the Delaware River from Philadelphia, was a major industrial center (Campbell Soup, RCA Victor, New York Shipbuilding). The county has deep ties to Philadelphia — many families crossed the river regularly. Walt Whitman spent his final years in Camden. The county includes both urban and suburban communities with distinct record-keeping histories.
county_metadata.research_tips: Camden County Clerk holds deeds from 1844; for earlier records check Gloucester County. The Surrogate has probate from 1844. The Camden County Historical Society at Pomona Hall (1726 house) in Camden has extensive manuscript collections, family files, and industrial records. For Camden City research, the Camden Free Public Library has city directories from the 1850s and extensive photograph collections. Many Camden families have Philadelphia connections — check the Historical Society of Pennsylvania and Philadelphia City Archives. RCA Victor and Campbell Soup company records document thousands of immigrant workers. The African American community in Camden is documented through church records (Kaighn Avenue Baptist Church from 1885) and the Camden County NAACP files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed and mortgage records from 1844; online search portal available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from county formation. Camden City property records especially valuable for tracking immigrant settlement patterns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1844.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, and guardianships from county formation.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1844.

source_profiles.source_name: Camden City Board of Health — Death Records
source_profiles.repository: Camden City Clerk / NJ State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal death records for Camden City, supplementing state vital records. Include cause of death and burial location.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival with some gaps in earliest years.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1850 (first after formation). Camden City industrial wards show diverse immigrant populations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Naturalization petitions from county courts. Significant for Irish, Italian, Polish, and German immigrants.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. Some early records transferred to state archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Camden Courier-Post and Camden Daily Telegram
source_profiles.repository: Camden County Historical Society / Camden Free Public Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Major Camden newspapers with obituaries, marriage notices, court proceedings, and industrial news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good microfilm coverage. Partial digitization.

county_metadata.record_losses: Minimal losses. A 1850 courthouse fire destroyed some early court records, but deed books and most probate records survived as they were in a separate vault.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the earliest NJ counties, organized 1692 from southern West Jersey. Cape May was a resort destination from the early 1800s — America's oldest seaside resort. The county's small population and geographic isolation preserved a distinctive Yankee-descended culture well into the 19th century. Whaling and fishing communities along the coast have unique occupational records. Dennis Township and Upper Township contain the oldest settlement areas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cape May County Clerk at Cape May Court House has deeds from 1692, though some early volumes are fragile. The Surrogate has probate from the 1690s. The Cape May County Museum and Genealogical Society (Cape May Court House) maintains extensive family files for the county's old families — the Ludlams, Townsends, Spikers, Corsons, Hands, and Leamings. The 'Cape May County Magazine of History and Genealogy' (published since 1931) is an outstanding resource. For whaling-era families, check ship crew lists at the Cape May County Historical Museum. Cape May City's Victorian resort era is documented in hotel registers and boarding house records at the Mid-Atlantic Center for the Arts. The county's isolation means many families intermarried extensively — prepare for complex cousin relationships.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed records from 1692; some early indexes available through county clerk

source_profiles.source_name: Cape May County Clerk — Deed Records from 1692
source_profiles.repository: Cape May County Clerk, Cape May Court House
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Continuous deed series from county organization. Early deeds reference West Jersey proprietary grants.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival. Early volumes carefully preserved despite 1850 courthouse fire.

source_profiles.source_name: Cape May County Surrogate — Probate Records
source_profiles.repository: Cape May County Surrogate, Cape May Court House
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills and administrations from county formation. Early wills reference fishing, whaling, and maritime occupations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. Some early volumes restored.

source_profiles.repository: NJ State Archives / Cape May County Clerk
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage bonds, licenses, and certificates. Cape May's small community means most marriages connected established families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1795. Earlier marriages in church records.

source_profiles.repository: Cape May County Museum and Genealogical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Transcriptions of cemetery inscriptions from across Cape May County including Cold Spring Presbyterian, Dias Creek, Seaville, and numerous small family burial grounds.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Comprehensive transcription project by the genealogical society. Many stones now illegible — transcriptions are primary source.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1800. Small population makes complete enumeration review feasible. Maritime occupations prominently listed.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890.

source_profiles.repository: Cold Spring Presbyterian Church / NJ State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: One of the oldest churches in Cape May County. Records include baptisms, marriages, burials, and session minutes documenting community life.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved. Key source for early Cape May families.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Cumberland County records are well preserved from county formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Salem County in 1748. Cumberland has Swedish, Finnish, and English Quaker settlement heritage from the 1600s. Bridgeton and Millville were glass-manufacturing centers attracting immigrant labor. The Maurice River oyster industry brought African American workers and watermen families. Greenwich was a major colonial port — the 'Greenwich Tea Burning' of 1774 paralleled the Boston Tea Party. Vineland was founded as a planned community by Charles K. Landis in 1861, attracting Italian immigrants.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cumberland County Clerk in Bridgeton holds deeds from 1748; for earlier records check Salem County. The Surrogate has probate from 1748. The Cumberland County Historical Society in Greenwich has outstanding manuscript collections for the colonial period. For Swedish heritage families, consult the Swedish Colonial Society records and Gloria Dei (Old Swedes') Church records in Philadelphia. The Vineland Historical and Antiquarian Society documents the Landis colony and Italian immigration. Millville's glass industry records are at the Museum of American Glass at WheatonArts. The Bayshore Heritage Byway communities along the Delaware Bay have unique maritime and oystering records. Check the NJ State Archives for Cumberland County road returns, tavern licenses, and slave registrations.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed and mortgage records from 1748; contact county clerk for access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from county formation. Early deeds reference West Jersey proprietary surveys.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1748.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, and inventories from county formation. Early wills document farming, maritime, and glass-making families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1748.

source_profiles.source_name: Greenwich Presbyterian Church and Quaker Meeting Records
source_profiles.repository: Cumberland County Historical Society / Friends Historical Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records from colonial-era congregations in Greenwich and surrounding communities. Greenwich Presbyterian records are among the oldest in South Jersey.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. Some records transcribed in published county histories.

source_profiles.source_name: Cumberland County Slave Birth Registry and Manumission Records
source_profiles.repository: NJ State Archives / Cumberland County Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave birth registrations required under the 1804 Gradual Abolition Act. Manumission deeds recorded with the county clerk. Cumberland County had significant slaveholding in the colonial era.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered but important records. Some published in NJ Archives series.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of Charles K. Landis's planned community and subsequent Italian agricultural colony. Church records, society records, and agricultural cooperative documents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved at the Vineland society.

source_profiles.repository: NJ State Archives / NJ Department of Health
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Death records from NJ statewide registration. Includes cause of death data useful for occupational health research (glass workers).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1848.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse fires. However, some Newark municipal records were damaged during the 1967 Newark riots, and some early township records have gaps.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of the four original East Jersey counties (1683). Newark, founded by Puritans from Connecticut in 1666, became NJ's largest city and a major industrial center. Essex County was reduced when Union County was created in 1857 and Passaic in 1837. Essex is the most densely populated county outside of Hudson. The county saw massive waves of immigration: Irish (1840s+), Germans (1850s+), Italians (1880s+), Jews (1890s+), African Americans (Great Migration, 1910s+), and later Portuguese, Brazilian, and Caribbean immigrants.
county_metadata.research_tips: Essex County Register of Deeds in Newark holds records from 1683 — one of the oldest deed series in NJ. The Surrogate has probate from the same period. The New Jersey Historical Society (NJHS) in Newark is the state's premier manuscript repository with family papers, business records, and maps dating to the colonial period. The Newark Public Library's Charles F. Cummings New Jersey Information Center has city directories from 1835, vital records indexes, and extensive photograph collections. For immigrant research, check parish records at the Catholic Archdiocese of Newark Archives (established 1853). The Jewish Historical Society of New Jersey documents the large Jewish community in Newark, the Oranges, and surrounding towns. For African American genealogy, consult the Institute of Jazz Studies at Rutgers-Newark and the Newark Museum archives for community documentation.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online deed index and document search; images available for recent decades; older records in-person

source_profiles.source_name: Essex County Register of Deeds — Records from 1683
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete deed and mortgage records from original county formation. Online index available. Newark property records are especially extensive.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival from 1683.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships from original county formation. Newark industrialist estates often contain detailed inventories.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1683.

source_profiles.repository: Newark City Clerk / NJ State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal birth, marriage, and death records for Newark. Pre-1848 events in church records only.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1848. Some gaps in earliest years.

source_profiles.repository: NJ State Archives / NARA New York
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Naturalization petitions from Essex County courts. Critical for documenting the massive immigration to Newark and surrounding industrial towns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. Federal court naturalizations at NARA.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete federal census. Newark's industrial wards show extraordinary ethnic diversity from 1870 onward.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890.

source_profiles.source_name: Archdiocese of Newark Catholic Parish Records
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Newark Archives / Individual parishes
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Sacramental registers from dozens of national parishes (Irish, Italian, Polish, German, Slovak, Portuguese) in Newark and Essex County. Each ethnic group typically had its own parish.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Generally well preserved. Closed parish records transferred to archdiocesan archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Newark Evening News and Newark Star-Ledger
source_profiles.repository: Newark Public Library / NJ Digital Highway
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Newark's major newspapers with extensive obituary sections, court notices, and immigrant community coverage. The Newark Daily Advertiser (from 1832) is one of the oldest.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Extensive microfilm runs at Newark Public Library. Growing digitization.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. Gloucester County records are well preserved from the colonial period.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Burlington County in 1686. Gloucester has Swedish colonial heritage — Fort Nassau (1626) and later Swedesboro settlements predate English control. The county lost territory to create Atlantic County (1837) and Camden County (1844). Woodbury became the county seat in 1787. The county straddles Swedish West Jersey and English Quaker settlement zones. Red Bank Battlefield (1777) is a significant Revolutionary War site within the county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Gloucester County Clerk in Woodbury holds deeds from 1686. The Surrogate has probate from the same era. For Swedish colonial families, consult the records of Trinity (Old Swedes') Church in Swedesboro (est. 1703) and the Swedish Colonial Society. The Gloucester County Historical Society in Woodbury has extensive manuscript collections, family files, and the 'Genealogical Magazine of New Jersey' back issues. For Quaker families, check Woodbury Monthly Meeting records at Friends Historical Library, Swarthmore. Pre-1686 records are in Burlington County. The NJ State Archives has Gloucester County road returns and tavern licenses from the colonial era. Deptford Township records predate county formation. Always check Philadelphia records for cross-river connections — Gloucester was the ferry crossing point.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed and mortgage records from county formation; some online index access

source_profiles.source_name: Gloucester County Clerk — Deed Records from 1686
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Continuous deed series from county formation. Some early transactions reference Swedish-era land claims.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival from 1686.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, inventories, and administrations from county formation. Early inventories reflect agricultural and maritime economy.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1686.

source_profiles.repository: Trinity Church, Swedesboro / Swedish Colonial Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptism, marriage, and burial records from one of the oldest Swedish Lutheran congregations in America. Records bridge the Swedish colonial and English periods.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved. Transcriptions published by the Swedish Colonial Society.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1790. Includes Swedish-surname families persisting from colonial settlement.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890.

source_profiles.repository: NJ State Archives / Gloucester County Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Militia rolls, oaths of allegiance, and damage claims. Red Bank Battlefield (1777) records document local participants.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. Published in NJ Archives series.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax lists showing property holders, livestock, acreage, and enslaved persons. Essential pre-vital-records source.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered but valuable. Best runs from 1770s-1810s.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major courthouse losses, but some Jersey City municipal records were damaged in a 1979 city hall fire. County-level records at the county courthouse remained intact.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Bergen County in 1840. Hudson County is the smallest NJ county by area but was long the most densely populated county in the United States. Jersey City, Hoboken, Bayonne, and Union City are the major municipalities. Hudson was the primary gateway for immigrants — Ellis Island is partially in NJ (confirmed by Supreme Court in 1998). The county had enormous ethnic diversity: Irish, German, Italian, Polish, Cuban, Filipino, and Indian communities. The waterfront was dominated by shipping, railroads, and manufacturing. Political machine history (Frank Hague era, 1917-1947) is significant.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hudson County Register of Deeds in Jersey City holds records from 1840; for earlier records check Bergen County. The Surrogate has probate from 1840. The Jersey City Free Public Library's New Jersey Room has city directories from the 1850s, photograph collections, and Frank Hague-era municipal records. The Hoboken Historical Museum has records of the Stevens family and early Hoboken. For immigration research, Ellis Island records are paramount — the Statue of Liberty-Ellis Island Foundation database covers 1892-1954. Pre-Ellis Island arrivals through Castle Garden (1855-1890) are at CastleGarden.org. Bayonne's Polish community is documented through St. Mary's Star of the Sea Church records. The Hudson County Genealogical and Historical Society provides research assistance. Many Hudson County residents worked in New York City — check Manhattan employment records for cross-river workers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed and mortgage records from 1840; online search available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from county formation. Dense urban property transactions make grantor/grantee indexes essential.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1840.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Passenger arrival records for immigrants processed through Ellis Island. Many gave Hudson County addresses as final destinations. Ellis Island is partially in NJ waters.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent. Fully digitized and searchable.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, and guardianships from county formation. Urban probate records often list specific addresses and occupations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1840.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1850 (first after formation). Jersey City and Hoboken enumeration districts show extreme population density and diversity.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890.

source_profiles.repository: NJ State Archives / NARA New York
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Massive volume of naturalization records from Hudson County courts reflecting the immigrant gateway status. Includes declarations, petitions, and certificates.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. One of the largest NJ naturalization record sets.

source_profiles.source_name: Jersey City and Hoboken Catholic Parish Records
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Newark Archives / Individual parishes
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Sacramental records from Irish, Italian, Polish, German, and Slovak national parishes. St. Peter's (Jersey City, est. 1831) is one of the oldest Catholic parishes in NJ.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Generally well preserved. Closed parish records at archdiocesan archives.

source_profiles.source_name: Jersey Journal and Hoboken Observer Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Jersey City Free Public Library / NJ Digital Highway
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Major Hudson County newspapers with daily obituaries, marriage notices, court proceedings, and ethnic community news.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good microfilm runs. Partial digitization.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Hunterdon's rural character helped preserve records. Some early court minute books have water damage but remain legible.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created from Burlington County in 1714 — one of the earliest county formations in NJ. Hunterdon originally covered a vast area of northwestern NJ. Morris County was taken from it in 1739, and Sussex County was taken from Morris in 1753. Hunterdon has a strong German (Palatine) immigration heritage from the 1720s-1740s, alongside earlier Dutch and English Quaker settlement. The Lindbergh baby kidnapping trial (1935) was held in Flemington. Hunterdon remains one of NJ's most rural counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hunterdon County Clerk in Flemington holds deeds from 1714; for earlier records check Burlington County. The Surrogate has probate from the same period. The Hunterdon County Historical Society in Flemington has outstanding genealogical resources including the Hiram E. Deats collection (one of the most extensive NJ genealogical manuscript collections ever assembled). For German Palatine families, check Reformed church records at the Gardner Sage Library, New Brunswick Theological Seminary. The Frenchtown-area Huguenot settlement is documented in French Reformed Church records. Clinton Historical Museum has local industrial records. Tax ratables at the NJ State Archives are critical for the colonial period. Hunterdon Deed Book A (1714) is one of the most genealogically important volumes in NJ — it contains early grants and transfers for all of northwestern NJ.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed and mortgage records from 1714; contact county clerk for access details

source_profiles.source_name: Hunterdon County Clerk — Deed Records from 1714
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed Book A covers the entire northwestern NJ region before Morris and Sussex were created. Critical for tracing early land ownership patterns.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Excellent survival from 1714.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, and inventories from county formation. German Palatine wills often list extensive family connections.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1714.

source_profiles.source_name: Hunterdon County Reformed and Lutheran Church Records
source_profiles.repository: Gardner Sage Library, New Brunswick / NJ State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: German Reformed and Lutheran congregation records from Palatine settlement communities. Includes baptisms, marriages, and burials. Some records in German script.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. Key records transcribed in the Genealogical Magazine of NJ.

source_profiles.source_name: Hunterdon County Tax Ratables and Assessment Lists
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial and early republic tax assessment lists. Show property holders, acreage, livestock, and enslaved persons. Essential pre-vital-records source for Hunterdon families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival for colonial period. Among the best tax record runs in NJ.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete federal census from 1790. German and Dutch surnames prominent in early enumerations.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890.

source_profiles.repository: NJ State Archives / Hunterdon County Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Militia muster rolls, oaths of allegiance, and damage claims. Hunterdon played a critical role in the Trenton-Princeton campaign of 1776-1777.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival. Published in NJ Archives series.

county_metadata.record_losses: No significant record losses. As the state capital county, Mercer has benefited from proximity to the NJ State Archives.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created in 1838 from parts of Burlington, Hunterdon, and Middlesex Counties — a rare tri-county formation. Named for General Hugh Mercer, killed at the Battle of Princeton (1777). Trenton, the state capital since 1790, is the county seat. Princeton University (est. 1746) and the Institute for Advanced Study give the county academic resources. Trenton was a major ceramics and wire rope manufacturing center. The Battles of Trenton (1776) and Princeton (1777) were turning points of the Revolution.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mercer County Clerk in Trenton holds deeds from 1838; for earlier records, you must check Burlington, Hunterdon, AND Middlesex Counties depending on the township. The Surrogate has probate from 1838. The NJ State Archives (NJSA) is in Trenton itself — the single most important genealogical repository in the state. The Trenton Free Public Library's Trentoniana Room has city directories from the 1840s and local history materials. Princeton University's Mudd Manuscript Library has colonial-era papers. The New Jersey State Museum has historical artifacts and documents. For Trenton ceramics industry workers, check Trenton Potteries Company records and related industrial archives. The Old Barracks Museum in Trenton has Revolutionary War resources. Mercer County's tri-county origin means you must identify which parent county held jurisdiction over your ancestor's township before 1838.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed and mortgage records from 1838; online index available

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and mortgage records from county formation. Trenton urban properties and Princeton-area estates both represented.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1838.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, and guardianships from county formation.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1838.

source_profiles.source_name: NJ State Archives — Mercer County Court Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Court of Common Pleas and Quarter Sessions records. Include criminal proceedings, civil suits, and naturalization records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved at the state archives.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1840. Trenton industrial wards and Princeton academic community provide contrasting demographics.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete except 1890.

source_profiles.source_name: Battle of Trenton and Princeton — Revolutionary War Records
source_profiles.repository: NJ State Archives / Old Barracks Museum
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records relating to the pivotal Battles of Trenton (Dec 1776) and Princeton (Jan 1777). Militia rolls, damage claims, and Hessian prisoner records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival at state archives and historical sites.

source_profiles.source_name: Trenton Times and State Gazette Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Trenton Free Public Library / NJ Digital Highway
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State capital newspapers with legislative coverage, obituaries, court notices, and Trenton community news. The NJ State Gazette dates to 1799.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good microfilm coverage. Growing digital access.
